reasoning:
ECB assets are experiencing a structural long-term decline due to Quantitative Tightening (QT), rolling off APP and PEPP portfolios. This puts downward pressure on the balance sheet at an average rate of roughly 20-30B EUR per month (or around 5-7B EUR per week). However, there is significant short-term weekly noise, and roughly 35-40% of standard weeks show a slight tick up in assets despite the overall trend. Furthermore, the Eurosystem re-evaluates its gold and foreign exchange assets at the start of every quarter. These revaluations (as seen in Jan, Apr, and especially Oct 2025) can lead to massive jumps (+50B to +138B) that overpower the QT runoff for that period.

1. 2025-11-02 (1 week out): Resolving against late October data, this represents just a 1-week change. Because weekly volatility frequently overcomes the small weekly QT drop, there is a moderate (~38%) chance of the value ticking up.
2. 2025-11-25 (4 weeks out): Over a 1-month period within a quarter (no revaluation), the cumulative QT effect almost entirely dominates weekly noise. Every 4-week non-reval window in the past year resulted in a net decrease. The chance of a net increase here is very low (~14%). (Note: Uncertainty decreases here relative to 1 week out because the deterministic trend overcomes the noise). 
3. 2026-01-24 (3 months out): This period spans the January Q1 revaluation. The QT trend would ordinarily pull the value down significantly (~60-90B), but a positive gold/FX revaluation could easily offset this (as the +138B jump did in October 2025). This makes the outcome roughly a coin toss, reflecting appropriately high uncertainty for the longest horizon.
